channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Interfacer
item.description:
An interfacer is a component that sits at a system boundary and transforms data between the system’s internal representation and an external format. Every system that communicates with the outside world has interfacers, whether or not it names them.

¶The two directions

An interfacer does exactly two things:

  1. Extract — read data from the external format into internal representation. A markdown parser extracts structured data from text files. A JSON deserializer extracts objects from strings. A database driver extracts rows from wire protocol. The external world comes in through extract.

item.title: Context Spacesuit
item.description: A context spacesuit is the predetermined package of system prompt, rules, skills, agent definitions, memory, and session state loaded into a language model’s context window before it begins work. The suit determines what the agent can perceive, what tools it can use, what constraints it operates under, and what history it carries. Remove the suit and the agent drifts inert. Change the suit and the same task space becomes a different world.

item.title: high availability
item.description:
High availability (HA) is a system design principle: the system continues providing service even when individual components fail. It is achieved through redundancy — running multiple instances of critical components so that when one fails, another takes over.

The standard measure is uptime percentage over a year:

Availability Downtime per year Common name
99% 3.65 days “two nines”
99.9% 8.76 hours “three nines”
99.99% 52.6 minutes “four nines”
99.999% 5.26 minutes “five nines”

Each additional nine requires roughly an order of magnitude more engineering effort and cost. Most small organizations target three nines (99.9%), which allows about 9 hours of downtime per year.

item.title: infrastructure as code
item.description:
Infrastructure as code (IaC) is the practice of defining computing infrastructure — servers, networks, databases, DNS records, firewall rules — in machine-readable configuration files rather than through manual setup or interactive consoles. The configuration files are version-controlled, reviewed, tested, and applied programmatically.

The idea is simple: if your infrastructure exists only as a sequence of manual steps someone performed, it cannot be reliably reproduced, audited, or moved. If it exists as code, it can be.

item.title: latency
item.description:
Latency is the time elapsed between initiating an action and receiving the result. In computing, it most commonly refers to network latency — the round-trip time for a packet to travel from one machine to another and back. It is measured in milliseconds (ms).

Latency is distinct from bandwidth. Bandwidth is the volume of data a connection can carry per unit time (measured in megabits per second). Latency is the delay before data arrives regardless of volume. A connection with high bandwidth and high latency can transfer large files quickly once the transfer starts, but each individual request still waits for the round-trip delay. For interactive work — keystrokes, editor commands, file operations — latency matters more than bandwidth because every action requires a round trip.

item.title: software container
item.description:
A software container is a unit of OS-level virtualization that packages an application together with its dependencies — libraries, runtime, configuration — into an isolated environment that runs on a shared operating system kernel. Unlike a virtual machine, which virtualizes an entire computer (hardware, OS kernel, and all), a container shares the host OS kernel and virtualizes only the userspace.

The practical consequence: containers start in milliseconds (no OS boot), use minimal overhead (no duplicate kernel), and are portable — a container built on one Linux machine runs on any other Linux machine with a compatible container runtime.

item.title: terminal multiplexer
item.description:
A terminal multiplexer is a program that manages multiple terminal sessions inside a single terminal connection. The two widely used implementations are tmux (2007–present, BSD license) and GNU Screen (1987–present, GPL).

A terminal multiplexer provides three capabilities that a raw terminal does not:

Session persistence. A session continues running on the server after the client disconnects. The user can reconnect later — from the same machine or a different one — and find the session exactly as they left it. All running processes, open files, and terminal output are preserved. Sessions survive network interruptions, client reboots, and switching between devices.

item.title: vendor lock-in
item.description:
Vendor lock-in is a condition where a customer depends on a vendor’s proprietary technology to the point where switching to an alternative would be prohibitively expensive, time-consuming, or risky. The dependency is created not by contract (though contracts may reinforce it) but by technical coupling — the customer’s systems use interfaces, formats, or services that only one vendor provides.

In computing, lock-in arises from three sources:

Proprietary APIs. When an application is built on a vendor-specific service (AWS Lambda, Google BigQuery, Azure Cosmos DB), the application’s code contains calls to that vendor’s API. Migrating to another provider requires rewriting every integration point. The more services used, the deeper the coupling.

item.title: virtual private server
item.description:
A virtual private server (VPS) is a virtual machine running on shared physical hardware, sold as a service by a hosting provider. The customer gets full root access to an isolated operating system instance — typically Linux — and pays a recurring fee based on allocated CPU, memory, and storage.

The “virtual” means the machine is a software partition of a larger physical server, managed by a hypervisor. The “private” means the customer’s instance is isolated from other tenants on the same hardware — each VPS has its own filesystem, processes, and network stack. The “server” means it runs continuously and is accessible over the network, typically via SSH.

item.title: JSON Schema
item.description:
JSON Schema is a declarative language for describing the structure of JSON data. A schema specifies what properties an object must have, what types those properties must be, which are required, what values are permitted, and how nested structures are composed. It is defined as an Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) draft specification, with the 2020-12 release being the current stable version.

A JSON Schema document is itself JSON. A simple example describing a function call:

item.title: Type Safety
item.description: Type safety is the property of a program or system that guarantees operations are only applied to data of compatible types. A type-safe system prevents you from treating a string as a number, passing three arguments to a function that expects two, or accessing a field that does not exist on a given data structure. The guarantee can be enforced at compile time (static type checking), at runtime (dynamic type checking), or at the boundary between systems (schema validation).

item.title: graphics processing unit
item.description:
A graphics processing unit (GPU) is a hardware accelerator built for massively parallel computation. Originally designed for rendering graphics — transforming 3D geometry into 2D images by performing the same operation on many data points simultaneously — GPUs have been repurposed for any workload that benefits from data parallelism, most notably machine learning.

A GPU’s architecture consists of thousands of small processing cores organized into groups that execute the same instruction on different data (SIMD/SIMT). This makes GPUs effective at matrix multiplication, which is the dominant operation in both graphics rendering and neural network computation. However, this capability comes at high power cost: a laptop GPU draws 30–50 watts under load, and datacenter GPUs draw 200–400 watts.

item.title: hardware accelerator
item.description:
A hardware accelerator is a specialized processor designed to perform a specific class of computations more efficiently than a general-purpose digital computer’s CPU. Efficiency here means some combination of higher throughput, lower latency, or lower power consumption for the target workload.

The general pattern is: identify a computation that a CPU performs by executing many generic instructions, then build circuitry that performs that computation directly. The trade-off is generality for efficiency — an accelerator that excels at matrix multiplication cannot run arbitrary programs.

item.title: neural processing unit
item.description:
A neural processing unit (NPU) is a hardware accelerator designed for neural network inference — the process of running a trained model on input data to produce predictions. Its circuitry is optimized for the multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations that dominate neural network computation, with on-chip memory arranged to minimize the data movement that bottlenecks general-purpose processors.

The distinguishing characteristic of an NPU compared to a GPU is power efficiency. Both can perform the parallel matrix arithmetic that neural networks require, but an NPU typically draws 0.5–3 watts during active inference versus 30–50 watts for a laptop GPU doing equivalent work. This makes NPUs suitable for sustained, always-on AI workloads — background noise cancellation, image segmentation in video calls, keyword detection — without significant battery impact.

item.title: Policy as code for knowledge repositories
item.description:
Policy as code (PaC) is the practice of expressing governance rules as machine-readable files that tools evaluate and enforce automatically. The pattern is well-established in infrastructure engineering (cloud permissions, deployment pipelines, code linting) but has not been applied to knowledge repositories where AI agents operate under discipline-specific constraints. This text surveys the relevant patterns and identifies what a knowledge repository can borrow.

¶The decision architecture

Every PaC system separates three concerns:

item.title: digital computer
item.description:
A digital computer is a machine that operates on discrete (digital) representations of information. It accepts input, processes it according to stored instructions, and produces output — all by manipulating sequences of binary digits. This definition covers everything from a pocket calculator to a supercomputer cluster, but the machines most relevant here are general-purpose programmable computers: devices whose behavior is determined by software rather than fixed circuitry.

Digital computers are tools for managing information and connections. They translate thoughts and commands into computational operations and return results that extend what a person can do alone. Through this relationship, we expand our capabilities to communicate, learn, create virtual environments, and relate more fully with both the material world and the realm of ideas.
